title: BBC founded while sex between men remains illegal in Britain
description: The British Broadcasting Company was established in 1922. At the time sex between men was a criminal offence in the United Kingdom and LGBT people lived largely hidden lives, while there was no specific law addressing relationships between women.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Ethel Smyth's music first broadcast by the BBC
description: The composer Ethel Smyth, who had many relationships with women and wrote the suffragette anthem 'March of the Women', had her work broadcast by the BBC and was honoured for her 75th birthday. She was the first woman appointed a Dame for services to music.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Gay policeman Harry Daley broadcasts on BBC radio
description: Harry Daley, a gay London police officer, spoke in the 'My Day's Work' slot on the BBC's Children's Hour. He had been brought to broadcasting by the BBC producer J. R. Ackerley and moved in a literary circle that included E. M. Forster.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Douglas Byng is the first female impersonator on television
description: Douglas Byng, a cabaret performer, appeared as the first female impersonator on BBC television. Openly gay within theatrical circles but discreet in public, he went on to host his own programmes.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Bandleader Ken 'Snakehips' Johnson killed in a London air raid
description: Ken 'Snakehips' Johnson, a Guyana-born swing bandleader who broadcast for the BBC and lived with the writer Gerald Hamilton, was killed by a bomb while performing at the Café de Paris in London during the Blitz.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Rex Jameson debuts the drag act Mrs Shufflewick on the BBC
description: The performer Rex Jameson created the drag character Mrs Shufflewick at a BBC audition and appeared on BBC radio and television from 1950 into the 1970s, later becoming a fixture of London's gay scene.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Actor John Gielgud arrested for a homosexual offence
description: The recently knighted actor John Gielgud was arrested for 'cottaging' in London. The case was one of several high-profile prosecutions of the early 1950s that helped prompt a review of the laws on homosexuality.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: BBC memos debate whether homosexuality can be discussed on air
description: Internal BBC memos from November 1953 recorded the corporation's division over whether sexual orientation could be addressed in its programmes, with the subject at first deemed unacceptable before being reconsidered days later.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Alan Turing dies after conviction for homosexuality
description: The mathematician and computing pioneer Alan Turing died from cyanide poisoning, recorded as suicide. In 1952 he had been convicted of gross indecency and accepted chemical treatment intended to suppress his sexuality rather than go to prison. He received a posthumous royal pardon in 2013.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Wolfenden Report recommends decriminalising sex between men
description: The Wolfenden Committee published its report on the laws covering homosexuality and prostitution, recommending that sex between men over 21 in private should no longer be a criminal offence. It took a decade for Parliament to change the law.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Lesbian broadcaster Naomi Jacob dies
description: Naomi Jacob, a writer, performer and broadcaster who lived openly as a lesbian and had defended Radclyffe Hall's 'The Well of Loneliness', died shortly after making her final BBC broadcast on Woman's Hour.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Polari reaches millions through Julian and Sandy on the BBC
description: The radio comedy 'Round the Horne' introduced the camp characters Julian and Sandy, played by Hugh Paddick and Kenneth Williams, who spoke the gay slang Polari to millions of Sunday listeners.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: England and Wales partially decriminalise sex between men
description: The Sexual Offences Act 1967 partially decriminalised sex between two men over 21 in private in England and Wales. It did not extend to Scotland, Northern Ireland, the Merchant Navy or the armed forces, and kept an unequal age of consent.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: First male same-sex kiss on British television
description: The BBC broadcast of Christopher Marlowe's 'Edward II' showed Ian McKellen as Edward kissing James Laurenson as Gaveston, regarded as the first male same-sex kiss on British television.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: First female same-sex kiss on British television
description: In the BBC adaptation of Jean-Paul Sartre's 'The Roads to Freedom', characters played by Alison Fiske and Consuela Chapman shared what is regarded as the first female same-sex kiss on British television.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Composer Angela Morley transitions and continues her BBC career
description: The composer and arranger previously known as Wally Stott, celebrated for radio comedies such as The Goons, transitioned in 1972 and, as Angela Morley, went on to an award-winning career that included conducting the BBC Concert Orchestra.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Trans community gets its own BBC programme for the first time
description: In the BBC's 'Open Door' access slot, members of a trans liberation group were given full editorial control over a programme about the issues affecting them, the first such trans-led broadcast in the UK.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: First on-screen kiss between characters written as lesbians in the UK
description: The BBC drama 'Girl', set in the army, showed a kiss between two characters explicitly written as lesbians, played by Alison Steadman and Myra Frances.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Jan Morris publishes Conundrum, an account of her transition
description: The historian and travel writer Jan Morris published 'Conundrum', describing her gender transition, and became one of the most prominent trans women in British public life.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Tom Robinson's 'Glad to be Gay' is released and banned by Radio 1
description: Tom Robinson released 'Glad to be Gay', reaching the Top 40 while openly gay at a time when the music industry expected artists to conceal their sexuality. Radio 1's chart show banned the song, though John Peel continued to play it.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: BBC documentary follows Julia Grant's gender transition
description: A BBC documentary series followed Julia Grant's gender transition, and the corporation's audience research found that only a minority of viewers reacted negatively to it.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: AIDS first reported in depth on British television
description: The BBC's 'Panorama' covered AIDS during a segment on sexually transmitted diseases, noting twelve AIDS-related deaths in the UK. It was among the first in-depth British television reporting on the emerging health crisis.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: The BBC bans Frankie Goes to Hollywood's 'Relax'
description: As openly gay pop acts broke into the charts, the BBC banned Frankie Goes to Hollywood's 'Relax' from daytime radio and chart shows after a Radio 1 DJ called it obscene, though some late-night DJs kept playing it.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: First same-sex kiss in a UK television soap
description: EastEnders showed the first same-sex kiss in a British soap opera when the character Colin gave his boyfriend Barry a kiss on the forehead, prompting hostile tabloid coverage.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Ian McKellen comes out on BBC radio
description: During a Radio 3 debate about Section 28, the actor Ian McKellen came out publicly. He went on to help found the LGBT rights charity Stonewall.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Section 28 becomes law in the United Kingdom
description: Section 28 of the Local Government Act 1988 barred local authorities from 'promoting' homosexuality, the first anti-LGBT law introduced in Britain in more than a century. It was repealed across the UK by 2003.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Activists storm the BBC Six O'Clock News over Section 28
description: Booan Temple and a group of lesbian campaigners broke through BBC security and disrupted a live broadcast of the Six O'Clock News to protest against Section 28, as newsreaders continued over the commotion.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Justin Fashanu becomes the first English footballer to come out
description: Justin Fashanu, the first black footballer to command a million-pound transfer fee, publicly came out as gay, the first professional footballer in England to do so. He faced abuse from crowds and took his own life in 1998.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: First national UK radio news magazine for gay listeners launches
description: 'Out This Week' began on BBC Radio Five Live, the first national radio news magazine made for lesbians and gay men. Presenter Nigel Wrench later used a Sony Award speech to come out as HIV positive.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Gaytime TV launches as the BBC's first LGBT television series
description: 'Gaytime TV' began on BBC Two, the first BBC television programme aimed at an LGBT audience, presented by Rhona Cameron, Bert Tyler-Moore and Richard Fairbrass. It ran until 1999.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Waheed Alli becomes the first openly gay member of the House of Lords
description: The media entrepreneur Waheed Alli became the youngest and first openly gay member of the House of Lords, and the most prominent gay politician from a Muslim background in Britain.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Ban on LGBT personnel in the UK armed forces is lifted
description: The United Kingdom lifted its ban on lesbian, gay and bisexual people serving in the armed forces, allowing them to serve openly for the first time.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: United Kingdom equalises the age of consent at 16
description: The age of consent for sex between men was equalised with that for heterosexual couples at 16 across the United Kingdom, ending a long-standing legal disparity.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: BBC adapts Tipping the Velvet for television
description: The BBC broadcast an adaptation of Sarah Waters' novel 'Tipping the Velvet', set in the Victorian music hall, pushing the boundaries of depictions of lesbian relationships on British television.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Section 28 is abolished in England, Wales and Northern Ireland
description: Section 28, which had barred local authorities from 'promoting' homosexuality, was repealed in England, Wales and Northern Ireland. Scotland had already abolished it in 2000.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Gender Recognition Act gives trans people legal recognition in the UK
description: The Gender Recognition Act took effect, allowing trans people in the United Kingdom to obtain legal recognition of their gender and an updated birth certificate.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Civil partnerships are introduced across the United Kingdom
description: The United Kingdom introduced civil partnerships, giving same-sex couples legal recognition and rights broadly comparable to marriage.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: The UK holds its first LGBT History Month
description: The United Kingdom's first LGBT History Month took place, gaining early publicity on BBC Radio 4's 'Today' programme.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: UK Equality Act extends protection in goods and services
description: Regulations extended protection against discrimination on grounds of sexual orientation to the provision of goods, facilities and services in the United Kingdom.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Rebecca Root is the first trans woman to lead a BBC comedy
description: Rebecca Root became the first trans woman cast to lead a British television comedy in 'Boy Meets Girl', which grew out of the BBC's Trans Comedy Award.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: First trans man to play a trans character in a UK soap
description: Riley Carter Millington joined EastEnders as Kyle Slater, becoming the first trans man to play a trans character in a British soap opera.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: The BBC marks 50 years of decriminalisation with Gay Britannia
description: The BBC's 'Gay Britannia' season marked the 50th anniversary of the 1967 Sexual Offences Act with programming exploring LGBT history and contemporary lives.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: Stephanie Hirst becomes the UK's first trans daily radio host
description: The broadcaster Stephanie Hirst, who came out as trans on BBC 5 Live, became the first trans DJ to host a daily radio show in the United Kingdom.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: The BBC appoints its first LGBT correspondent
description: Ben Hunte was appointed the BBC's first LGBT correspondent, a role dedicated to covering LGBT issues in its news output.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: First all-male dance partnership appears on Strictly Come Dancing
description: Johannes Radebe and Graziano Di Prima performed as the first all-male partnership on 'Strictly Come Dancing'. The BBC upheld none of the roughly 300 complaints about their appearance.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: RuPaul's Drag Race UK premieres on the BBC
description: 'RuPaul's Drag Race UK' launched on BBC Three, drawing millions of streams and contributing to the channel's return as a broadcast service.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: The BBC marks 50 years of Pride in the UK
description: The BBC marked the 50th anniversary of the first UK Pride with a season of television and radio programming across its channels and stations.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: BBC documentary exposes entrapment of LGBT people in Egypt
description: A BBC News investigation by Ahmed Shihab-Eldin culminated in a documentary revealing how Egyptian police used dating apps to entrap and prosecute LGBT people under a 'debauchery' law.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: The BBC renews its queer dating series I Kissed a Girl
description: The BBC renewed 'I Kissed a Girl' for a second series with Dannii Minogue as host, alongside the continuing 'I Kissed a Boy', reality dating shows centred on LGBT contestants.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)

title: CBeebies introduces a children's show with a two-dad family
description: The animated series 'Rafi the Wishing Wizard' launched on CBeebies, following a young wizard who lives in Manchester with her two dads, presenting an LGBT-parent family in mainstream children's programming.

sources.label: BBC — LGBTQ+ Timeline (History of the BBC)
